interpretation_summary: The replication completed successfully after resolving initial package-compatibility errors and adding a lightweight Python driver file.  The preregistered analysis – a participant-clustered logistic regression of binary utilitarian judgements on mortality-salience condition, dilemma type and their interaction – was executed on an online 2021 sample (n≈258 observations, 130 participants).  Whereas the original laboratory experiments (n=85) reported a statistically significant reduction in utilitarian responses under mortality salience (Death) and a significant Salience × Scenario interaction (F(1,83)=6.89, p=.01; 55 % vs 39 %), the replication produced (1) a negative, non-significant coefficient for the pain (control) condition relative to death (b=-0.375, SE=0.443, p=.398), meaning the direction is opposite to that predicted, and (2) a non-significant positive interaction with scenario (b=0.761, SE=0.702, p=.279).  Thus, the replication does not corroborate the focal claim; if anything, utilitarian acceptability was slightly higher – not lower – under mortality salience, although the difference is well within sampling error.  Methodologically, the replication followed the preregistered plan closely, with minor deviations (logistic model vs. ANOVA; two dilemmas instead of four; different participant pool).

fidelity_assessment.method_alignment: High alignment: The code implemented a clustered logistic regression on the preregistered variables (mortality-salience × dilemma type) and saved the coefficient table, matching the plan.  The data were filtered with the same manipulation-check rule described in the plan.

deviations.issue_description: Statistical model differs from original (logistic GLM on binary responses vs. ANOVA on proportions).
deviations.impact: Low–moderate: Should not change direction or significance in large samples; preserves hypothesis test but effect size units differ.

deviations.issue_description: Replication dataset contains only two dilemma variants not the four used in the original Experiment 1.
deviations.impact: Moderate: Reduces within-subject variation and may lower power to detect the interaction.

results_comparison.hypothesis_tested: Participants primed with mortality salience (death writing task) will show LOWER utilitarian acceptability in moral-conflict dilemmas than participants in a pain (control) condition.
results_comparison.original_results: Interaction of Salience × Scenario significant: F(1,83)=6.89, p=.01, η²_p=0.08.  Utilitarian acceptability on conflict dilemmas: Pain 55 % vs Death 39 % (Δ≈+16 pp in predicted direction).
results_comparison.replication_results: Coefficient for pain vs death on conflict-scenario baseline: b=-0.375, SE=0.443, 95 % CI [-1.243, 0.494], p=.398 (direction opposite to predicted).  Interaction coefficient (pain × partial-beneficience): b=0.761, SE=0.702, 95 % CI [-0.616, 2.137], p=.279 (non-significant).  Pseudo-R²=0.058.
results_comparison.overall_answer: No – the replication fails to meet the preregistered criterion (effect not significant and direction reversed).

replication_report: Replication unsuccessful: mortality-salience prime did not reduce utilitarian responding; coefficient non-significant and opposite in sign to the original effect.

failure_handling.suggestions: Maintain consistent NumPy/Statsmodels binary versions in template images to avoid build-time incompatibilities.
Provide an ANOVA analysis in addition to logistic regression to mirror the original metric; include all four dilemma variants where possible.

notes: The online sample and altered stimulus set may limit comparability.  Power analysis suggests ~120 participants affords ~70 % power to detect η²_p=0.08; nevertheless, direction reversal undermines the claim.  Future work could examine whether cultural context or pandemic-era mortality salience moderates the effect.
